title: Aeneid, Book 1
source: Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th c.) via Vatican Library IIIF + Perseus Digital Library Latin (Greenough ed.) + Dryden English (1697, PG #228)
description: Book 1 of Virgil's Aeneid. Manuscript folio from Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225), the oldest surviving illustrated manuscript of Virgil (4th-5th century AD), held at the Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana. Latin text verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain). English translation verbatim from John Dryden's verse rendering (1697, Project Gutenberg #228, public domain).
introduction:
This entry presents Book 1 of Virgil's Aeneid with the strict three-criteria standard of the Theosis Library:

  1. Manuscript scan: A folio from the Vergilius Vaticanus (Cod. Vat. lat. 3225, 4th-5th century), one of the oldest and most important surviving Virgil manuscripts, with painted illustrations in the late antique Roman style. Via Vatican Library IIIF (digi.vatlib.it).
  2. Original text: The complete Latin text of Aeneid Book 1 (756 lines), verbatim from the Greenough edition via Perseus Digital Library (public domain).
  3. English translation: John Dryden's celebrated verse translation (1697), verbatim from Project Gutenberg #228 (public domain).

translation.original_text: <sup>1</sup>Arma virumque cano, Troiae qui primus ab oris <sup>2</sup>Italiam, fato profugus, Laviniaque venit <sup>3</sup>litora, multum ille et terris iactatus et alto <sup>4</sup>vi superum saevae memorem Iunonis ob iram; <sup>5</sup>multa quoque et bello passus, dum conderet urbem, <sup>6</sup>inferretque deos Latio, genus unde Latinum, <sup>7</sup>Albanique patres, atque altae moenia Romae. <sup>8</sup>Musa, mihi causas memora, quo numine laeso, <sup>9</sup>quidve dolens, regina deum tot volvere casus <sup>10</sup>insignem pietate virum, tot adire labores <sup>11</sup>impulerit. Tantaene animis caelestibus irae? <sup>12</sup>Urbs antiqua fuit, Tyrii tenuere coloni, <sup>13</sup>Karthago, Italiam contra Tiberinaque longe <sup>14</sup>ostia, dives opum studiisque asperrima belli; <sup>15</sup>quam Iuno fertur terris magis omnibus unam <sup>16</sup>posthabita coluisse Samo; hic illius arma, <sup>17</sup>hic currus fuit; hoc regnum dea gentibus esse, <sup>18</sup>si qua fata sinant, iam tum tenditque fovetque. <sup>19</sup>Progeniem sed enim Troiano a sanguine duci <sup>20</sup>audierat, Tyrias olim quae verteret arces; <sup>21</sup>hinc populum late regem belloque superbum <sup>22</sup>venturum excidio Libyae: sic volvere Parcas. <sup>23</sup>Id metuens, veterisque memor Saturnia belli, <sup>24</sup>prima quod ad Troiam pro caris gesserat Argis— <sup>25</sup>necdum etiam causae irarum saevique dolores
translation.text: The Trojans, after a seven years’ voyage, set sail for Italy, but are overtaken by a dreadful storm, which Aeolus raises at the request of Juno. The tempest sinks one, and scatters the rest. Neptune drives off the winds, and calms the sea. Aeneas, with his own ship and six more, arrives safe at an African port. Venus complains to Jupiter of her son’s misfortunes. Jupiter comforts her, and sends Mercury to procure him a kind reception among the Carthaginians. Aeneas, going out to discover the country, meets his mother in the shape of a huntress, who conveys him in a cloud to Carthage, where he sees his friends whom he thought lost, and receives a kind entertainment from the queen. Dido, by device of Venus, begins to have a passion for him, and, after some discourse with him, desires the history of his adventures since the siege of Troy, which is the subject of the two following books.

translation.original_text: <sup>26</sup>exciderant animo: manet alta mente repostum <sup>27</sup>iudicium Paridis spretaeque iniuria formae, <sup>28</sup>et genus invisum, et rapti Ganymedis honores. <sup>29</sup>His accensa super, iactatos aequore toto <sup>30</sup>Troas, reliquias Danaum atque immitis Achilli, <sup>31</sup>arcebat longe Latio, multosque per annos <sup>32</sup>errabant, acti fatis, maria omnia circum. <sup>33</sup>Tantae molis erat Romanam condere gentem! <sup>34</sup>Vix e conspectu Siculae telluris in altum <sup>35</sup>vela dabant laeti, et spumas salis aere ruebant, <sup>36</sup>cum Iuno, aeternum servans sub pectore volnus, <sup>37</sup>haec secum: Mene incepto desistere victam, <sup>38</sup>nec posse Italia Teucrorum avertere regem? <sup>39</sup>Quippe vetor fatis. Pallasne exurere classem <sup>40</sup>Argivum atque ipsos potuit submergere ponto, <sup>41</sup>unius ob noxam et furias Aiacis Oilei? <sup>42</sup>Ipsa, Iovis rapidum iaculata e nubibus ignem, <sup>43</sup>disiecitque rates evertitque aequora ventis, <sup>44</sup>illum expirantem transfixo pectore flammas <sup>45</sup>turbine corripuit scopuloque infixit acuto. <sup>46</sup>Ast ego, quae divom incedo regina, Iovisque <sup>47</sup>et soror et coniunx, una cum gente tot annos <sup>48</sup>bella gero!  Et quisquam numen Iunonis adoret <sup>49</sup>praeterea, aut supplex aris imponet honorem? <sup>50</sup>Talia flammato secum dea corde volutans
translation.text: Arms, and the man I sing, who, forc’d by fate, And haughty Juno’s unrelenting hate, Expell’d and exil’d, left the Trojan shore. Long labours, both by sea and land, he bore, And in the doubtful war, before he won The Latian realm, and built the destin’d town; His banish’d gods restor’d to rites divine, And settled sure succession in his line, From whence the race of Alban fathers come, And the long glories of majestic Rome. O Muse! the causes and the crimes relate; What goddess was provok’d, and whence her hate; For what offence the Queen of Heav’n began To persecute so brave, so just a man; Involv’d his anxious life in endless cares, Expos’d to wants, and hurried into wars! Can heav’nly minds such high resentment show, Or exercise their spite in human woe?

translation.original_text: <sup>51</sup>nimborum in patriam, loca feta furentibus austris, <sup>52</sup>Aeoliam venit. Hic vasto rex Aeolus antro <sup>53</sup>luctantes ventos tempestatesque sonoras <sup>54</sup>imperio premit ac vinclis et carcere frenat. <sup>55</sup>Illi indignantes magno cum murmure montis <sup>56</sup>circum claustra fremunt; celsa sedet Aeolus arce <sup>57</sup>sceptra tenens, mollitque animos et temperat iras. <sup>58</sup>Ni faciat, maria ac terras caelumque profundum <sup>59</sup>quippe ferant rapidi secum verrantque per auras. <sup>60</sup>Sed pater omnipotens speluncis abdidit atris, <sup>61</sup>hoc metuens, molemque et montis insuper altos <sup>62</sup>imposuit, regemque dedit, qui foedere certo <sup>63</sup>et premere et laxas sciret dare iussus habenas. <sup>64</sup>Ad quem tum Iuno supplex his vocibus usa est: <sup>65</sup>Aeole, namque tibi divom pater atque hominum rex <sup>66</sup>et mulcere dedit fluctus et tollere vento, <sup>67</sup>gens inimica mihi Tyrrhenum navigat aequor, <sup>68</sup>Ilium in Italiam portans victosque Penates: <sup>69</sup>incute vim ventis submersasque obrue puppes, <sup>70</sup>aut age diversos et disiice corpora ponto. <sup>71</sup>Sunt mihi bis septem praestanti corpore nymphae, <sup>72</sup>quarum quae forma pulcherrima Deiopea, <sup>73</sup>conubio iungam stabili propriamque dicabo, <sup>74</sup>omnis ut tecum meritis pro talibus annos <sup>75</sup>exigat, et pulchra faciat te prole parentem.
translation.text: Against the Tiber’s mouth, but far away, An ancient town was seated on the sea; A Tyrian colony; the people made Stout for the war, and studious of their trade: Carthage the name; belov’d by Juno more Than her own Argos, or the Samian shore. Here stood her chariot; here, if Heav’n were kind, The seat of awful empire she design’d. Yet she had heard an ancient rumour fly, (Long cited by the people of the sky,) That times to come should see the Trojan race Her Carthage ruin, and her tow’rs deface; Nor thus confin’d, the yoke of sov’reign sway Should on the necks of all the nations lay. She ponder’d this, and fear’d it was in fate; Nor could forget the war she wag’d of late For conqu’ring Greece against the Trojan state. Besides, long causes working in her mind, And secret seeds of envy, lay behind; Deep graven in her heart the doom remain’d Of partial Paris, and her form disdain’d; The grace bestow’d on ravish’d Ganymed, Electra’s glories, and her injur’d bed. Each was a cause alone; and all combin’d To kindle vengeance in her haughty mind. For this, far distant from the Latian coast She drove the remnants of the Trojan host; And sev’n long years th’ unhappy wand’ring train Were toss’d by storms, and scatter’d thro’ the main. Such time, such toil, requir’d the Roman name, Such length of labour for so vast a frame.

translation.original_text: <sup>76</sup>Aeolus haec contra: Tuus, O regina, quid optes <sup>77</sup>explorare labor; mihi iussa capessere fas est. <sup>78</sup>Tu mihi, quodcumque hoc regni, tu sceptra Iovemque <sup>79</sup>concilias, tu das epulis accumbere divom, <sup>80</sup>nimborumque facis tempestatumque potentem. <sup>81</sup>Haec ubi dicta, cavum conversa cuspide montem <sup>82</sup>impulit in latus: ac venti, velut agmine facto, <sup>83</sup>qua data porta, ruunt et terras turbine perflant. <sup>84</sup>Incubuere mari, totumque a sedibus imis <sup>85</sup>una Eurusque Notusque ruunt creberque procellis <sup>86</sup>Africus, et vastos volvunt ad litora fluctus. <sup>87</sup>Insequitur clamorque virum stridorque rudentum. <sup>88</sup>Eripiunt subito nubes caelumque diemque <sup>89</sup>Teucrorum ex oculis; ponto nox incubat atra. <sup>90</sup>Intonuere poli, et crebris micat ignibus aether, <sup>91</sup>praesentemque viris intentant omnia mortem. <sup>92</sup>Extemplo Aeneae solvuntur frigore membra: <sup>93</sup>ingemit, et duplicis tendens ad sidera palmas <sup>94</sup>talia voce refert: O terque quaterque beati, <sup>95</sup>quis ante ora patrum Troiae sub moenibus altis <sup>96</sup>contigit oppetere! O Danaum fortissime gentis <sup>97</sup>Tydide! Mene Iliacis occumbere campis <sup>98</sup>non potuisse, tuaque animam hanc effundere dextra, <sup>99</sup>saevus ubi Aeacidae telo iacet Hector, ubi ingens <sup>100</sup>Sarpedon, ubi tot Simois correpta sub undis
translation.text:
Now scarce the Trojan fleet, with sails and oars, Had left behind the fair Sicilian shores, Ent’ring with cheerful shouts the wat’ry reign, And plowing frothy furrows in the main; When, lab’ring still with endless discontent, The Queen of Heav’n did thus her fury vent:

“Then am I vanquish’d? must I yield?” said she, “And must the Trojans reign in Italy? So Fate will have it, and Jove adds his force; Nor can my pow’r divert their happy course. Could angry Pallas, with revengeful spleen, The Grecian navy burn, and drown the men? She, for the fault of one offending foe, The bolts of Jove himself presum’d to throw: With whirlwinds from beneath she toss’d the ship, And bare expos’d the bosom of the deep; Then, as an eagle gripes the trembling game, The wretch, yet hissing with her father’s flame, She strongly seiz’d, and with a burning wound Transfix’d, and naked, on a rock she bound. But I, who walk in awful state above, The majesty of heav’n, the sister wife of Jove, For length of years my fruitless force employ Against the thin remains of ruin’d Troy! What nations now to Juno’s pow’r will pray, Or off’rings on my slighted altars lay?”

translation.original_text: <sup>101</sup>scuta virum galeasque et fortia corpora volvit? <sup>102</sup>Talia iactanti stridens Aquilone procella <sup>103</sup>velum adversa ferit, fluctusque ad sidera tollit. <sup>104</sup>Franguntur remi; tum prora avertit, et undis <sup>105</sup>dat latus; insequitur cumulo praeruptus aquae mons. <sup>106</sup>Hi summo in fluctu pendent; his unda dehiscens <sup>107</sup>terram inter fluctus aperit; furit aestus harenis. <sup>108</sup>Tris Notus abreptas in saxa latentia torquet— <sup>109</sup>saxa vocant Itali mediis quae in fluctibus aras— <sup>110</sup>dorsum immane mari summo; tris Eurus ab alto <sup>111</sup>in brevia et Syrtis urguet, miserabile visu, <sup>112</sup>inliditque vadis atque aggere cingit harenae. <sup>113</sup>Unam, quae Lycios fidumque vehebat Oronten, <sup>114</sup>ipsius ante oculos ingens a vertice pontus <sup>115</sup>in puppim ferit: excutitur pronusque magister <sup>116</sup>volvitur in caput; ast illam ter fluctus ibidem <sup>117</sup>torquet agens circum, et rapidus vorat aequore vortex. <sup>118</sup>Adparent rari nantes in gurgite vasto, <sup>119</sup>arma virum, tabulaeque, et Troia gaza per undas. <sup>120</sup>Iam validam Ilionei navem, iam fortis Achati, <sup>121</sup>et qua vectus Abas, et qua grandaevus Aletes, <sup>122</sup>vicit hiems; laxis laterum compagibus omnes <sup>123</sup>accipiunt inimicum imbrem, rimisque fatiscunt. <sup>124</sup>Interea magno misceri murmure pontum, <sup>125</sup>emissamque hiemem sensit Neptunus, et imis
translation.text: Thus rag’d the goddess; and, with fury fraught. The restless regions of the storms she sought, Where, in a spacious cave of living stone, The tyrant Aeolus, from his airy throne, With pow’r imperial curbs the struggling winds, And sounding tempests in dark prisons binds. This way and that th’ impatient captives tend, And, pressing for release, the mountains rend. High in his hall th’ undaunted monarch stands, And shakes his scepter, and their rage commands; Which did he not, their unresisted sway Would sweep the world before them in their way; Earth, air, and seas thro’ empty space would roll, And heav’n would fly before the driving soul. In fear of this, the Father of the Gods Confin’d their fury to those dark abodes, And lock’d ’em safe within, oppress’d with mountain loads; Impos’d a king, with arbitrary sway, To loose their fetters, or their force allay. To whom the suppliant queen her pray’rs address’d, And thus the tenor of her suit express’d:

translation.original_text: <sup>126</sup>stagna refusa vadis, graviter commotus; et alto <sup>127</sup>prospiciens, summa placidum caput extulit unda. <sup>128</sup>Disiectam Aeneae, toto videt aequore classem, <sup>129</sup>fluctibus oppressos Troas caelique ruina, <sup>130</sup>nec latuere doli fratrem Iunonis et irae. <sup>131</sup>Eurum ad se Zephyrumque vocat, dehinc talia fatur: <sup>132</sup>Tantane vos generis tenuit fiducia vestri? <sup>133</sup>Iam caelum terramque meo sine numine, venti, <sup>134</sup>miscere, et tantas audetis tollere moles? <sup>135</sup>Quos ego—sed motos praestat componere fluctus. <sup>136</sup>Post mihi non simili poena commissa luetis. <sup>137</sup>Maturate fugam, regique haec dicite vestro: <sup>138</sup>non illi imperium pelagi saevumque tridentem, <sup>139</sup>sed mihi sorte datum. Tenet ille immania saxa, <sup>140</sup>vestras, Eure, domos; illa se iactet in aula <sup>141</sup>Aeolus, et clauso ventorum carcere regnet. <sup>142</sup>Sic ait, et dicto citius tumida aequora placat, <sup>143</sup>collectasque fugat nubes, solemque reducit. <sup>144</sup>Cymothoe simul et Triton adnixus acuto <sup>145</sup>detrudunt navis scopulo; levat ipse tridenti; <sup>146</sup>et vastas aperit syrtis, et temperat aequor, <sup>147</sup>atque rotis summas levibus perlabitur undas. <sup>148</sup>Ac veluti magno in populo cum saepe coorta est <sup>149</sup>seditio, saevitque animis ignobile volgus, <sup>150</sup>iamque faces et saxa volant—furor arma ministrat;
translation.text: “O Aeolus! for to thee the King of Heav’n The pow’r of tempests and of winds has giv’n; Thy force alone their fury can restrain, And smooth the waves, or swell the troubled main. A race of wand’ring slaves, abhorr’d by me, With prosp’rous passage cut the Tuscan sea; To fruitful Italy their course they steer, And for their vanquish’d gods design new temples there. Raise all thy winds; with night involve the skies; Sink or disperse my fatal enemies. Twice sev’n, the charming daughters of the main, Around my person wait, and bear my train: Succeed my wish, and second my design; The fairest, Deiopeia, shall be thine, And make thee father of a happy line.”

translation.original_text: <sup>151</sup>tum, pietate gravem ac meritis si forte virum quem <sup>152</sup>conspexere, silent, arrectisque auribus adstant; <sup>153</sup>ille regit dictis animos, et pectora mulcet,— <sup>154</sup>sic cunctus pelagi cecidit fragor, aequora postquam <sup>155</sup>prospiciens genitor caeloque invectus aperto <sup>156</sup>flectit equos, curruque volans dat lora secundo. <sup>157</sup>Defessi Aeneadae, quae proxima litora, cursu <sup>158</sup>contendunt petere, et Libyae vertuntur ad oras. <sup>159</sup>Est in secessu longo locus: insula portum <sup>160</sup>efficit obiectu laterum, quibus omnis ab alto <sup>161</sup>frangitur inque sinus scindit sese unda reductos. <sup>162</sup>Hinc atque hinc vastae rupes geminique minantur <sup>163</sup>in caelum scopuli, quorum sub vertice late <sup>164</sup>aequora tuta silent; tum silvis scaena coruscis <sup>165</sup>desuper horrentique atrum nemus imminet umbra. <sup>166</sup>Fronte sub adversa scopulis pendentibus antrum, <sup>167</sup>intus aquae dulces vivoque sedilia saxo, <sup>168</sup>nympharum domus: hic fessas non vincula navis <sup>169</sup>ulla tenent, unco non alligat ancora morsu. <sup>170</sup>Huc septem Aeneas collectis navibus omni <sup>171</sup>ex numero subit; ac magno telluris amore <sup>172</sup>egressi optata potiuntur Troes harena, <sup>173</sup>et sale tabentis artus in litore ponunt. <sup>174</sup>Ac primum silici scintillam excudit Achates, <sup>175</sup>succepitque ignem foliis, atque arida circum
translation.text: To this the god: “’Tis yours, O queen, to will The work which duty binds me to fulfil. These airy kingdoms, and this wide command, Are all the presents of your bounteous hand: Yours is my sov’reign’s grace; and, as your guest, I sit with gods at their celestial feast; Raise tempests at your pleasure, or subdue; Dispose of empire, which I hold from you.”

translation.original_text: <sup>176</sup>nutrimenta dedit, rapuitque in fomite flammam. <sup>177</sup>Tum Cererem corruptam undis Cerealiaque arma <sup>178</sup>expediunt fessi rerum, frugesque receptas <sup>179</sup>et torrere parant flammis et frangere saxo. <sup>180</sup>Aeneas scopulum interea conscendit, et omnem <sup>181</sup>prospectum late pelago petit, Anthea si quem <sup>182</sup>iactatum vento videat Phrygiasque biremis, <sup>183</sup>aut Capyn, aut celsis in puppibus arma Caici. <sup>184</sup>Navem in conspectu nullam, tris litore cervos <sup>185</sup>prospicit errantis; hos tota armenta sequuntur <sup>186</sup>a tergo, et longum per vallis pascitur agmen. <sup>187</sup>Constitit hic, arcumque manu celerisque sagittas <sup>188</sup>corripuit, fidus quae tela gerebat Achates; <sup>189</sup>ductoresque ipsos primum, capita alta ferentis <sup>190</sup>cornibus arboreis, sternit, tum volgus, et omnem <sup>191</sup>miscet agens telis nemora inter frondea turbam; <sup>192</sup>nec prius absistit, quam septem ingentia victor <sup>193</sup>corpora fundat humi, et numerum cum navibus aequet. <sup>194</sup>Hinc portum petit, et socios partitur in omnes. <sup>195</sup>Vina bonus quae deinde cadis onerarat Acestes <sup>196</sup>litore Trinacrio dederatque abeuntibus heros, <sup>197</sup>dividit, et dictis maerentia pectora mulcet: <sup>198</sup>O socii—neque enim ignari sumus ante malorum— <sup>199</sup>O passi graviora, dabit deus his quoque finem. <sup>200</sup>Vos et Scyllaeam rabiem penitusque sonantis
translation.text:
He said, and hurl’d against the mountain side His quiv’ring spear, and all the god applied. The raging winds rush thro’ the hollow wound, And dance aloft in air, and skim along the ground; Then, settling on the sea, the surges sweep, Raise liquid mountains, and disclose the deep. South, East, and West with mix’d confusion roar, And roll the foaming billows to the shore. The cables crack; the sailors’ fearful cries Ascend; and sable night involves the skies; And heav’n itself is ravish’d from their eyes. Loud peals of thunder from the poles ensue; Then flashing fires the transient light renew; The face of things a frightful image bears, And present death in various forms appears. Struck with unusual fright, the Trojan chief, With lifted hands and eyes, invokes relief; And, “Thrice and four times happy those,” he cried, “That under Ilian walls before their parents died! Tydides, bravest of the Grecian train! Why could not I by that strong arm be slain, And lie by noble Hector on the plain, Or great Sarpedon, in those bloody fields Where Simois rolls the bodies and the shields Of heroes, whose dismember’d hands yet bear The dart aloft, and clench the pointed spear!”

Thus while the pious prince his fate bewails, Fierce Boreas drove against his flying sails, And rent the sheets; the raging billows rise, And mount the tossing vessels to the skies: Nor can the shiv’ring oars sustain the blow; The galley gives her side, and turns her prow; While those astern, descending down the steep, Thro’ gaping waves behold the boiling deep. Three ships were hurried by the southern blast, And on the secret shelves with fury cast. Those hidden rocks th’ Ausonian sailors knew: They call’d them Altars, when they rose in view, And show’d their spacious backs above the flood. Three more fierce Eurus, in his angry mood, Dash’d on the shallows of the moving sand, And in mid ocean left them moor’d a-land. Orontes’ bark, that bore the Lycian crew, (A horrid sight!) ev’n in the hero’s view, From stem to stern by waves was overborne: The trembling pilot, from his rudder torn, Was headlong hurl’d; thrice round the ship was toss’d, Then bulg’d at once, and in the deep was lost; And here and there above the waves were seen Arms, pictures, precious goods, and floating men. The stoutest vessel to the storm gave way, And suck’d thro’ loosen’d planks the rushing sea. Ilioneus was her chief: Alethes old, Achates faithful, Abas young and bold, Endur’d not less; their ships, with gaping seams, Admit the deluge of the briny streams.

translation.original_text: <sup>201</sup>accestis scopulos, vos et Cyclopea saxa <sup>202</sup>experti: revocate animos, maestumque timorem <sup>203</sup>mittite: forsan et haec olim meminisse iuvabit. <sup>204</sup>Per varios casus, per tot discrimina rerum <sup>205</sup>tendimus in Latium; sedes ubi fata quietas <sup>206</sup>ostendunt; illic fas regna resurgere Troiae. <sup>207</sup>Durate, et vosmet rebus servate secundis. <sup>208</sup>Talia voce refert, curisque ingentibus aeger <sup>209</sup>spem voltu simulat, premit altum corde dolorem. <sup>210</sup>Illi se praedae accingunt, dapibusque futuris; <sup>211</sup>tergora deripiunt costis et viscera nudant; <sup>212</sup>pars in frusta secant veribusque trementia figunt; <sup>213</sup>litore aena locant alii, flammasque ministrant. <sup>214</sup>Tum victu revocant vires, fusique per herbam <sup>215</sup>implentur veteris Bacchi pinguisque ferinae. <sup>216</sup>Postquam exempta fames epulis mensaeque remotae, <sup>217</sup>amissos longo socios sermone requirunt, <sup>218</sup>spemque metumque inter dubii, seu vivere credant, <sup>219</sup>sive extrema pati nec iam exaudire vocatos. <sup>220</sup>Praecipue pius Aeneas nunc acris Oronti, <sup>221</sup>nunc Amyci casum gemit et crudelia secum <sup>222</sup>fata Lyci, fortemque Gyan, fortemque Cloanthum. <sup>223</sup>Et iam finis erat, cum Iuppiter aethere summo <sup>224</sup>despiciens mare velivolum terrasque iacentis <sup>225</sup>litoraque et latos populos, sic vertice caeli
translation.text: Meantime imperial Neptune heard the sound Of raging billows breaking on the ground. Displeas’d, and fearing for his wat’ry reign, He rear’d his awful head above the main, Serene in majesty; then roll’d his eyes Around the space of earth, and seas, and skies. He saw the Trojan fleet dispers’d, distress’d, By stormy winds and wintry heav’n oppress’d. Full well the god his sister’s envy knew, And what her aims and what her arts pursue. He summon’d Eurus and the western blast, And first an angry glance on both he cast; Then thus rebuk’d: “Audacious winds! from whence This bold attempt, this rebel insolence? Is it for you to ravage seas and land, Unauthoriz’d by my supreme command? To raise such mountains on the troubled main? Whom I—but first ’tis fit the billows to restrain; And then you shall be taught obedience to my reign. Hence! to your lord my royal mandate bear, The realms of ocean and the fields of air Are mine, not his. By fatal lot to me The liquid empire fell, and trident of the sea. His pow’r to hollow caverns is confin’d: There let him reign, the jailer of the wind, With hoarse commands his breathing subjects call, And boast and bluster in his empty hall.” He spoke; and, while he spoke, he smooth’d the sea, Dispell’d the darkness, and restor’d the day. Cymothoe, Triton, and the sea-green train Of beauteous nymphs, the daughters of the main, Clear from the rocks the vessels with their hands: The god himself with ready trident stands, And opes the deep, and spreads the moving sands; Then heaves them off the shoals. Where’er he guides His finny coursers and in triumph rides, The waves unruffle and the sea subsides. As, when in tumults rise th’ ignoble crowd, Mad are their motions, and their tongues are loud; And stones and brands in rattling volleys fly, And all the rustic arms that fury can supply: If then some grave and pious man appear, They hush their noise, and lend a list’ning ear; He soothes with sober words their angry mood, And quenches their innate desire of blood: So, when the Father of the Flood appears, And o’er the seas his sov’reign trident rears, Their fury falls: he skims the liquid plains, High on his chariot, and, with loosen’d reins, Majestic moves along, and awful peace maintains. The weary Trojans ply their shatter’d oars To nearest land, and make the Libyan shores.

translation.original_text: <sup>226</sup>constitit, et Libyae defixit lumina regnis. <sup>227</sup>Atque illum talis iactantem pectore curas <sup>228</sup>tristior et lacrimis oculos suffusa nitentis <sup>229</sup>adloquitur Venus: O qui res hominumque deumque <sup>230</sup>aeternis regis imperiis, et fulmine terres, <sup>231</sup>quid meus Aeneas in te committere tantum, <sup>232</sup>quid Troes potuere, quibus, tot funera passis, <sup>233</sup>cunctus ob Italiam terrarum clauditur orbis? <sup>234</sup>Certe hinc Romanos olim, volventibus annis, <sup>235</sup>hinc fore ductores, revocato a sanguine Teucri, <sup>236</sup>qui mare, qui terras omni dicione tenerent, <sup>237</sup>pollicitus, quae te, genitor, sententia vertit? <sup>238</sup>Hoc equidem occasum Troiae tristisque ruinas <sup>239</sup>solabar, fatis contraria fata rependens; <sup>240</sup>nunc eadem fortuna viros tot casibus actos <sup>241</sup>insequitur. Quem das finem, rex magne, laborum? <sup>242</sup>Antenor potuit, mediis elapsus Achivis, <sup>243</sup>Illyricos penetrare sinus, atque intima tutus <sup>244</sup>regna Liburnorum, et fontem superare Timavi, <sup>245</sup>unde per ora novem vasto cum murmure montis <sup>246</sup>it mare proruptum et pelago premit arva sonanti. <sup>247</sup>Hic tamen ille urbem Patavi sedesque locavit <sup>248</sup>Teucrorum, et genti nomen dedit, armaque fixit <sup>249</sup>Troia; nunc placida compostus pace quiescit: <sup>250</sup>nos, tua progenies, caeli quibus adnuis arcem,
translation.text: Within a long recess there lies a bay: An island shades it from the rolling sea, And forms a port secure for ships to ride; Broke by the jutting land, on either side, In double streams the briny waters glide. Betwixt two rows of rocks a sylvan scene Appears above, and groves for ever green: A grot is form’d beneath, with mossy seats, To rest the Nereids, and exclude the heats. Down thro’ the crannies of the living walls The crystal streams descend in murm’ring falls: No haulsers need to bind the vessels here, Nor bearded anchors; for no storms they fear. Sev’n ships within this happy harbour meet, The thin remainders of the scatter’d fleet. The Trojans, worn with toils, and spent with woes, Leap on the welcome land, and seek their wish’d repose.

translation.original_text: <sup>251</sup>navibus (infandum!) amissis, unius ob iram <sup>252</sup>prodimur atque Italis longe disiungimur oris. <sup>253</sup>Hic pietatis honos? Sic nos in sceptra reponis? <sup>254</sup>Olli subridens hominum sator atque deorum, <sup>255</sup>voltu, quo caelum tempestatesque serenat, <sup>256</sup>oscula libavit natae, dehinc talia fatur: <sup>257</sup>Parce metu, Cytherea: manent immota tuorum <sup>258</sup>fata tibi; cernes urbem et promissa Lavini <sup>259</sup>moenia, sublimemque feres ad sidera caeli <sup>260</sup>magnanimum Aenean; neque me sententia vertit. <sup>261</sup>Hic tibi (fabor enim, quando haec te cura remordet, <sup>262</sup>longius et volvens fatorum arcana movebo) <sup>263</sup>bellum ingens geret Italia, populosque feroces <sup>264</sup>contundet, moresque viris et moenia ponet, <sup>265</sup>tertia dum Latio regnantem viderit aestas, <sup>266</sup>ternaque transierint Rutulis hiberna subactis. <sup>267</sup>At puer Ascanius, cui nunc cognomen Iulo <sup>268</sup>additur,—Ilus erat, dum res stetit Ilia regno,— <sup>269</sup>triginta magnos volvendis mensibus orbis <sup>270</sup>imperio explebit, regnumque ab sede Lavini <sup>271</sup>transferet, et longam multa vi muniet Albam. <sup>272</sup>Hic iam ter centum totos regnabitur annos <sup>273</sup>gente sub Hectorea, donec regina sacerdos, <sup>274</sup>Marte gravis, geminam partu dabit Ilia prolem. <sup>275</sup>Inde lupae fulvo nutricis tegmine laetus
translation.text: First, good Achates, with repeated strokes Of clashing flints, their hidden fire provokes: Short flame succeeds; a bed of wither’d leaves The dying sparkles in their fall receives: Caught into life, in fiery fumes they rise, And, fed with stronger food, invade the skies. The Trojans, dropping wet, or stand around The cheerful blaze, or lie along the ground: Some dry their corn, infected with the brine, Then grind with marbles, and prepare to dine. Aeneas climbs the mountain’s airy brow, And takes a prospect of the seas below, If Capys thence, or Antheus he could spy, Or see the streamers of Caicus fly. No vessels were in view; but, on the plain, Three beamy stags command a lordly train Of branching heads: the more ignoble throng Attend their stately steps, and slowly graze along. He stood; and, while secure they fed below, He took the quiver and the trusty bow Achates us’d to bear: the leaders first He laid along, and then the vulgar pierc’d; Nor ceas’d his arrows, till the shady plain Sev’n mighty bodies with their blood distain. For the sev’n ships he made an equal share, And to the port return’d, triumphant from the war. The jars of gen’rous wine (Acestes’ gift, When his Trinacrian shores the navy left) He set abroach, and for the feast prepar’d, In equal portions with the ven’son shar’d. Thus while he dealt it round, the pious chief With cheerful words allay’d the common grief: “Endure, and conquer! Jove will soon dispose To future good our past and present woes. With me, the rocks of Scylla you have tried; Th’ inhuman Cyclops and his den defied. What greater ills hereafter can you bear? Resume your courage and dismiss your care, An hour will come, with pleasure to relate Your sorrows past, as benefits of Fate. Thro’ various hazards and events, we move To Latium and the realms foredoom’d by Jove. Call’d to the seat (the promise of the skies) Where Trojan kingdoms once again may rise, Endure the hardships of your present state; Live, and reserve yourselves for better fate.”

translation.original_text: <sup>276</sup>Romulus excipiet gentem, et Mavortia condet <sup>277</sup>moenia, Romanosque suo de nomine dicet. <sup>278</sup>His ego nec metas rerum nec tempora pono; <sup>279</sup>imperium sine fine dedi. Quin aspera Iuno, <sup>280</sup>quae mare nunc terrasque metu caelumque fatigat, <sup>281</sup>consilia in melius referet, mecumque fovebit <sup>282</sup>Romanos rerum dominos gentemque togatam: <sup>283</sup>sic placitum. Veniet lustris labentibus aetas, <sup>284</sup>cum domus Assaraci Phthiam clarasque Mycenas <sup>285</sup>servitio premet, ac victis dominabitur Argis. <sup>286</sup>Nascetur pulchra Troianus origine Caesar, <sup>287</sup>imperium oceano, famam qui terminet astris,— <sup>288</sup>Iulius, a magno demissum nomen Iulo. <sup>289</sup>Hunc tu olim caelo, spoliis Orientis onustum, <sup>290</sup>accipies secura; vocabitur hic quoque votis. <sup>291</sup>Aspera tum positis mitescent saecula bellis; <sup>292</sup>cana Fides, et Vesta, Remo cum fratre Quirinus, <sup>293</sup>iura dabunt; dirae ferro et compagibus artis <sup>294</sup>claudentur Belli portae; Furor impius intus, <sup>295</sup>saeva sedens super arma, et centum vinctus aenis <sup>296</sup>post tergum nodis, fremet horridus ore cruento. <sup>297</sup>Haec ait, et Maia genitum demittit ab alto, <sup>298</sup>ut terrae, utque novae pateant Karthaginis arces <sup>299</sup>hospitio Teucris, ne fati nescia Dido <sup>300</sup>finibus arceret: volat ille per aera magnum
translation.text:
These words he spoke, but spoke not from his heart; His outward smiles conceal’d his inward smart. The jolly crew, unmindful of the past, The quarry share, their plenteous dinner haste. Some strip the skin; some portion out the spoil; The limbs, yet trembling, in the caldrons boil; Some on the fire the reeking entrails broil. Stretch’d on the grassy turf, at ease they dine, Restore their strength with meat, and cheer their souls with wine. Their hunger thus appeas’d, their care attends The doubtful fortune of their absent friends: Alternate hopes and fears their minds possess, Whether to deem ’em dead, or in distress. Above the rest, Aeneas mourns the fate Of brave Orontes, and th’ uncertain state Of Gyas, Lycus, and of Amycus. The day, but not their sorrows, ended thus.

When, from aloft, almighty Jove surveys Earth, air, and shores, and navigable seas, At length on Libyan realms he fix’d his eyes: Whom, pond’ring thus on human miseries, When Venus saw, she with a lowly look, Not free from tears, her heav’nly sire bespoke:

translation.original_text: <sup>301</sup>remigio alarum, ac Libyae citus adstitit oris. <sup>302</sup>Et iam iussa facit, ponuntque ferocia Poeni <sup>303</sup>corda volente deo; in primis regina quietum <sup>304</sup>accipit in Teucros animum mentemque benignam. <sup>305</sup>At pius Aeneas, per noctem plurima volvens, <sup>306</sup>ut primum lux alma data est, exire locosque <sup>307</sup>explorare novos, quas vento accesserit oras, <sup>308</sup>qui teneant, nam inculta videt, hominesne feraene, <sup>309</sup>quaerere constituit, sociisque exacta referre <sup>310</sup>Classem in convexo nemorum sub rupe cavata <sup>311</sup>arboribus clausam circum atque horrentibus umbris <sup>312</sup>occulit; ipse uno graditur comitatus Achate, <sup>313</sup>bina manu lato crispans hastilia ferro. <sup>314</sup>Cui mater media sese tulit obvia silva, <sup>315</sup>virginis os habitumque gerens, et virginis arma <sup>316</sup>Spartanae, vel qualis equos Threissa fatigat <sup>317</sup>Harpalyce, volucremque fuga praevertitur Hebrum. <sup>318</sup>Namque umeris de more habilem suspenderat arcum <sup>319</sup>venatrix, dederatque comam diffundere ventis, <sup>320</sup>nuda genu, nodoque sinus collecta fluentis. <sup>321</sup>Ac prior, Heus inquit iuvenes, monstrate mearum <sup>322</sup>vidistis si quam hic errantem forte sororum, <sup>323</sup>succinctam pharetra et maculosae tegmine lyncis, <sup>324</sup>aut spumantis apri cursum clamore prementem. <sup>325</sup>Sic Venus; et Veneris contra sic filius orsus:
translation.text: “O King of Gods and Men! whose awful hand Disperses thunder on the seas and land, Disposing all with absolute command; How could my pious son thy pow’r incense? Or what, alas! is vanish’d Troy’s offence? Our hope of Italy not only lost, On various seas by various tempests toss’d, But shut from ev’ry shore, and barr’d from ev’ry coast. You promis’d once, a progeny divine Of Romans, rising from the Trojan line, In after times should hold the world in awe, And to the land and ocean give the law. How is your doom revers’d, which eas’d my care When Troy was ruin’d in that cruel war? Then fates to fates I could oppose; but now, When Fortune still pursues her former blow, What can I hope? What worse can still succeed? What end of labours has your will decreed? Antenor, from the midst of Grecian hosts, Could pass secure, and pierce th’ Illyrian coasts, Where, rolling down the steep, Timavus raves And thro’ nine channels disembogues his waves. At length he founded Padua’s happy seat, And gave his Trojans a secure retreat; There fix’d their arms, and there renew’d their name, And there in quiet rules, and crown’d with fame. But we, descended from your sacred line, Entitled to your heav’n and rites divine, Are banish’d earth; and, for the wrath of one, Remov’d from Latium and the promis’d throne. Are these our scepters? these our due rewards? And is it thus that Jove his plighted faith regards?”

translation.original_text: <sup>326</sup>Nulla tuarum audita mihi neque visa sororum— <sup>327</sup>O quam te memorem, virgo? Namque haud tibi voltus <sup>328</sup>mortalis, nec vox hominem sonat: O, dea certe— <sup>329</sup>an Phoebi soror? an nympharum sanguinis una?— <sup>330</sup>sis felix, nostrumque leves, quaecumque, laborem, <sup>331</sup>et, quo sub caelo tandem, quibus orbis in oris <sup>332</sup>iactemur, doceas. Ignari hominumque locorumque <sup>333</sup>erramus, vento huc vastis et fluctibus acti: <sup>334</sup>multa tibi ante aras nostra cadet hostia dextra. <sup>335</sup>Tum Venus: Haud equidem tali me dignor honore; <sup>336</sup>virginibus Tyriis mos est gestare pharetram, <sup>337</sup>purpureoque alte suras vincire cothurno. <sup>338</sup>Punica regna vides, Tyrios et Agenoris urbem; <sup>339</sup>sed fines Libyci, genus intractabile bello. <sup>340</sup>Imperium Dido Tyria regit urbe profecta, <sup>341</sup>germanum fugiens. Longa est iniuria, longae <sup>342</sup>ambages; sed summa sequar fastigia rerum. <sup>343</sup>Huic coniunx Sychaeus erat, ditissimus agri <sup>344</sup>Phoenicum, et magno miserae dilectus amore, <sup>345</sup>cui pater intactam dederat, primisque iugarat <sup>346</sup>ominibus. Sed regna Tyri germanus habebat <sup>347</sup>Pygmalion, scelere ante alios immanior omnes. <sup>348</sup>Quos inter medius venit furor. Ille Sychaeum <sup>349</sup>impius ante aras, atque auri caecus amore, <sup>350</sup>clam ferro incautum superat, securus amorum
translation.text: To whom the Father of th’ immortal race, Smiling with that serene indulgent face, With which he drives the clouds and clears the skies, First gave a holy kiss; then thus replies:

translation.original_text: <sup>351</sup>germanae; factumque diu celavit, et aegram, <sup>352</sup>multa malus simulans, vana spe lusit amantem. <sup>353</sup>Ipsa sed in somnis inhumati venit imago <sup>354</sup>coniugis, ora modis attollens pallida miris, <sup>355</sup>crudeles aras traiectaque pectora ferro <sup>356</sup>nudavit, caecumque domus scelus omne retexit. <sup>357</sup>Tum celerare fugam patriaque excedere suadet, <sup>358</sup>auxiliumque viae veteres tellure recludit <sup>359</sup>thesauros, ignotum argenti pondus et auri. <sup>360</sup>His commota fugam Dido sociosque parabat: <sup>361</sup>conveniunt, quibus aut odium crudele tyranni <sup>362</sup>aut metus acer erat; navis, quae forte paratae, <sup>363</sup>corripiunt, onerantque auro: portantur avari <sup>364</sup>Pygmalionis opes pelago; dux femina facti. <sup>365</sup>Devenere locos, ubi nunc ingentia cernis <sup>366</sup>moenia surgentemque novae Karthaginis arcem, <sup>367</sup>mercatique solum, facti de nomine Byrsam, <sup>368</sup>taurino quantum possent circumdare tergo. <sup>369</sup>Sed vos qui tandem, quibus aut venistis ab oris, <sup>370</sup>quove tenetis iter? Quaerenti talibus ille <sup>371</sup>suspirans, imoque trahens a pectore vocem: <sup>372</sup>O dea, si prima repetens ab origine pergam, <sup>373</sup>et vacet annalis nostrorum audire laborum, <sup>374</sup>ante diem clauso componat Vesper Olympo. <sup>375</sup>Nos Troia antiqua, si vestras forte per auris
translation.text: “Daughter, dismiss thy fears; to thy desire The fates of thine are fix’d, and stand entire. Thou shalt behold thy wish’d Lavinian walls; And, ripe for heav’n, when fate Aeneas calls, Then shalt thou bear him up, sublime, to me: No councils have revers’d my firm decree. And, lest new fears disturb thy happy state, Know, I have search’d the mystic rolls of Fate: Thy son (nor is th’ appointed season far) In Italy shall wage successful war, Shall tame fierce nations in the bloody field, And sov’reign laws impose, and cities build, Till, after ev’ry foe subdued, the sun Thrice thro’ the signs his annual race shall run: This is his time prefix’d. Ascanius then, Now call’d Iulus, shall begin his reign. He thirty rolling years the crown shall wear, Then from Lavinium shall the seat transfer, And, with hard labour, Alba Longa build. The throne with his succession shall be fill’d Three hundred circuits more: then shall be seen Ilia the fair, a priestess and a queen, Who, full of Mars, in time, with kindly throes, Shall at a birth two goodly boys disclose. The royal babes a tawny wolf shall drain: Then Romulus his grandsire’s throne shall gain, Of martial tow’rs the founder shall become, The people Romans call, the city Rome. To them no bounds of empire I assign, Nor term of years to their immortal line. Ev’n haughty Juno, who, with endless broils, Earth, seas, and heav’n, and Jove himself turmoils; At length aton’d, her friendly pow’r shall join, To cherish and advance the Trojan line. The subject world shall Rome’s dominion own, And, prostrate, shall adore the nation of the gown. An age is ripening in revolving fate When Troy shall overturn the Grecian state, And sweet revenge her conqu’ring sons shall call, To crush the people that conspir’d her fall. Then Caesar from the Julian stock shall rise, Whose empire ocean, and whose fame the skies Alone shall bound; whom, fraught with eastern spoils, Our heav’n, the just reward of human toils, Securely shall repay with rites divine; And incense shall ascend before his sacred shrine. Then dire debate and impious war shall cease, And the stern age be soften’d into peace: Then banish’d Faith shall once again return, And Vestal fires in hallow’d temples burn; And Remus with Quirinus shall sustain The righteous laws, and fraud and force restrain. Janus himself before his fane shall wait, And keep the dreadful issues of his gate, With bolts and iron bars: within remains Imprison’d Fury, bound in brazen chains; High on a trophy rais’d, of useless arms, He sits, and threats the world with vain alarms.”

translation.original_text: <sup>376</sup>Troiae nomen iit, diversa per aequora vectos <sup>377</sup>forte sua Libycis tempestas adpulit oris. <sup>378</sup>Sum pius Aeneas, raptos qui ex hoste Penates <sup>379</sup>classe veho mecum, fama super aethera notus. <sup>380</sup>Italiam quaero patriam et genus ab Iove summo. <sup>381</sup>Bis denis Phrygium conscendi navibus aequor, <sup>382</sup>matre dea monstrante viam, data fata secutus; <sup>383</sup>vix septem convolsae undis Euroque supersunt. <sup>384</sup>Ipse ignotus, egens, Libyae deserta peragro, <sup>385</sup>Europa atque Asia pulsus. Nec plura querentem <sup>386</sup>passa Venus medio sic interfata dolore est: <sup>387</sup>Quisquis es, haud, credo, invisus caelestibus auras <sup>388</sup>vitalis carpis, Tyriam qui adveneris urbem. <sup>389</sup>Perge modo, atque hinc te reginae ad limina perfer, <sup>390</sup>Namque tibi reduces socios classemque relatam <sup>391</sup>nuntio, et in tutum versis aquilonibus actam, <sup>392</sup>ni frustra augurium vani docuere parentes. <sup>393</sup>Aspice bis senos laetantis agmine cycnos, <sup>394</sup>aetheria quos lapsa plaga Iovis ales aperto <sup>395</sup>turbabat caelo; nunc terras ordine longo <sup>396</sup>aut capere, aut captas iam despectare videntur: <sup>397</sup>ut reduces illi ludunt stridentibus alis, <sup>398</sup>et coetu cinxere polum, cantusque dedere, <sup>399</sup>haud aliter puppesque tuae pubesque tuorum <sup>400</sup>aut portum tenet aut pleno subit ostia velo.
translation.text:
He said, and sent Cyllenius with command To free the ports, and ope the Punic land To Trojan guests; lest, ignorant of fate, The queen might force them from her town and state. Down from the steep of heav’n Cyllenius flies, And cleaves with all his wings the yielding skies. Soon on the Libyan shore descends the god, Performs his message, and displays his rod: The surly murmurs of the people cease; And, as the fates requir’d, they give the peace: The queen herself suspends the rigid laws, The Trojans pities, and protects their cause.

Meantime, in shades of night Aeneas lies: Care seiz’d his soul, and sleep forsook his eyes. But, when the sun restor’d the cheerful day, He rose, the coast and country to survey, Anxious and eager to discover more. It look’d a wild uncultivated shore; But, whether humankind, or beasts alone Possess’d the new-found region, was unknown. Beneath a ledge of rocks his fleet he hides: Tall trees surround the mountain’s shady sides; The bending brow above a safe retreat provides. Arm’d with two pointed darts, he leaves his friends, And true Achates on his steps attends. Lo! in the deep recesses of the wood, Before his eyes his goddess mother stood: A huntress in her habit and her mien; Her dress a maid, her air confess’d a queen. Bare were her knees, and knots her garments bind; Loose was her hair, and wanton’d in the wind; Her hand sustain’d a bow; her quiver hung behind. She seem’d a virgin of the Spartan blood: With such array Harpalyce bestrode Her Thracian courser and outstripp’d the rapid flood. “Ho, strangers! have you lately seen,” she said, “One of my sisters, like myself array’d, Who cross’d the lawn, or in the forest stray’d? A painted quiver at her back she bore; Varied with spots, a lynx’s hide she wore; And at full cry pursued the tusky boar.”

translation.original_text: <sup>401</sup>Perge modo, et, qua te ducit via, dirige gressum. <sup>402</sup>Dixit, et avertens rosea cervice refulsit, <sup>403</sup>ambrosiaeque comae divinum vertice odorem <sup>404</sup>spiravere, pedes vestis defluxit ad imos, <sup>405</sup>et vera incessu patuit dea. Ille ubi matrem <sup>406</sup>adgnovit, tali fugientem est voce secutus: <sup>407</sup>Quid natum totiens, crudelis tu quoque, falsis <sup>408</sup>ludis imaginibus? Cur dextrae iungere dextram <sup>409</sup>non datur, ac veras audire et reddere voces? <sup>410</sup>Talibus incusat, gressumque ad moenia tendit: <sup>411</sup>at Venus obscuro gradientes aere saepsit, <sup>412</sup>et multo nebulae circum dea fudit amictu, <sup>413</sup>cernere ne quis eos, neu quis contingere posset, <sup>414</sup>molirive moram, aut veniendi poscere causas. <sup>415</sup>Ipsa Paphum sublimis abit, sedesque revisit <sup>416</sup>laeta suas, ubi templum illi, centumque Sabaeo <sup>417</sup>ture calent arae, sertisque recentibus halant. <sup>418</sup>Corripuere viam interea, qua semita monstrat. <sup>419</sup>Iamque ascendebant collem, qui plurimus urbi <sup>420</sup>imminet, adversasque adspectat desuper arces. <sup>421</sup>Miratur molem Aeneas, magalia quondam, <sup>422</sup>miratur portas strepitumque et strata viarum. <sup>423</sup>Instant ardentes Tyrii pars ducere muros, <sup>424</sup>molirique arcem et manibus subvolvere saxa, <sup>425</sup>pars optare locum tecto et concludere sulco.
translation.text: Thus Venus: thus her son replied again: “None of your sisters have we heard or seen, O virgin! or what other name you bear Above that style; O more than mortal fair! Your voice and mien celestial birth betray! If, as you seem, the sister of the day, Or one at least of chaste Diana’s train, Let not an humble suppliant sue in vain; But tell a stranger, long in tempests toss’d, What earth we tread, and who commands the coast? Then on your name shall wretched mortals call, And offer’d victims at your altars fall.” “I dare not,” she replied, “assume the name Of goddess, or celestial honours claim: For Tyrian virgins bows and quivers bear, And purple buskins o’er their ankles wear. Know, gentle youth, in Libyan lands you are: A people rude in peace, and rough in war. The rising city, which from far you see, Is Carthage, and a Tyrian colony. Phoenician Dido rules the growing state, Who fled from Tyre, to shun her brother’s hate. Great were her wrongs, her story full of fate; Which I will sum in short. Sichaeus, known For wealth, and brother to the Punic throne, Possess’d fair Dido’s bed; and either heart At once was wounded with an equal dart. Her father gave her, yet a spotless maid; Pygmalion then the Tyrian scepter sway’d: One who condemn’d divine and human laws. Then strife ensued, and cursed gold the cause. The monarch, blinded with desire of wealth, With steel invades his brother’s life by stealth; Before the sacred altar made him bleed, And long from her conceal’d the cruel deed. Some tale, some new pretence, he daily coin’d, To soothe his sister, and delude her mind. At length, in dead of night, the ghost appears Of her unhappy lord: the spectre stares, And, with erected eyes, his bloody bosom bares. The cruel altars and his fate he tells, And the dire secret of his house reveals, Then warns the widow, with her household gods, To seek a refuge in remote abodes. Last, to support her in so long a way, He shows her where his hidden treasure lay. Admonish’d thus, and seiz’d with mortal fright, The queen provides companions of her flight: They meet, and all combine to leave the state, Who hate the tyrant, or who fear his hate. They seize a fleet, which ready rigg’d they find; Nor is Pygmalion’s treasure left behind. The vessels, heavy laden, put to sea With prosp’rous winds; a woman leads the way. I know not, if by stress of weather driv’n, Or was their fatal course dispos’d by Heav’n; At last they landed, where from far your eyes May view the turrets of new Carthage rise; There bought a space of ground, which Byrsa call’d, From the bull’s hide, they first inclos’d, and wall’d. But whence are you? what country claims your birth? What seek you, strangers, on our Libyan earth?”

translation.original_text: <sup>426</sup>Iura magistratusque legunt sanctumque senatum; <sup>427</sup>hic portus alii effodiunt; hic alta theatris <sup>428</sup>fundamenta locant alii, immanisque columnas <sup>429</sup>rupibus excidunt, scaenis decora alta futuris. <sup>430</sup>Qualis apes aestate nova per florea rura <sup>431</sup>exercet sub sole labor, cum gentis adultos <sup>432</sup>educunt fetus, aut cum liquentia mella <sup>433</sup>stipant et dulci distendunt nectare cellas, <sup>434</sup>aut onera accipiunt venientum, aut agmine facto <sup>435</sup>ignavom fucos pecus a praesepibus arcent: <sup>436</sup>fervet opus, redolentque thymo fragrantia mella. <sup>437</sup>O fortunati, quorum iam moenia surgunt! <sup>438</sup>Aeneas ait, et fastigia suspicit urbis. <sup>439</sup>Infert se saeptus nebula, mirabile dictu, <sup>440</sup>per medios, miscetque viris, neque cernitur ulli. <sup>441</sup>Lucus in urbe fuit media, laetissimus umbra, <sup>442</sup>quo primum iactati undis et turbine Poeni <sup>443</sup>effodere loco signum, quod regia Iuno <sup>444</sup>monstrarat, caput acris equi; sic nam fore bello <sup>445</sup>egregiam et facilem victu per saecula gentem. <sup>446</sup>Hic templum Iunoni ingens Sidonia Dido <sup>447</sup>condebat, donis opulentum et numine divae, <sup>448</sup>aerea cui gradibus surgebant limina, nexaeque <sup>449</sup>aere trabes, foribus cardo stridebat aenis. <sup>450</sup>Hoc primum in luco nova res oblata timorem
translation.text: To whom, with sorrow streaming from his eyes, And deeply sighing, thus her son replies: “Could you with patience hear, or I relate, O nymph, the tedious annals of our fate! Thro’ such a train of woes if I should run, The day would sooner than the tale be done! From ancient Troy, by force expell’d, we came, If you by chance have heard the Trojan name. On various seas by various tempests toss’d, At length we landed on your Libyan coast. The good Aeneas am I call’d, a name, While Fortune favour’d, not unknown to fame. My household gods, companions of my woes, With pious care I rescued from our foes. To fruitful Italy my course was bent; And from the King of Heav’n is my descent. With twice ten sail I cross’d the Phrygian sea; Fate and my mother goddess led my way. Scarce sev’n, the thin remainders of my fleet, From storms preserv’d, within your harbour meet. Myself distress’d, an exile, and unknown, Debarr’d from Europe, and from Asia thrown, In Libyan deserts wander thus alone.”

translation.original_text: <sup>451</sup>leniit, hic primum Aeneas sperare salutem <sup>452</sup>ausus, et adflictis melius confidere rebus. <sup>453</sup>Namque sub ingenti lustrat dum singula templo, <sup>454</sup>reginam opperiens, dum, quae fortuna sit urbi, <sup>455</sup>artificumque manus inter se operumque laborem <sup>456</sup>miratur, videt Iliacas ex ordine pugnas, <sup>457</sup>bellaque iam fama totum volgata per orbem, <sup>458</sup>Atridas, Priamumque, et saevum ambobus Achillem. <sup>459</sup>Constitit, et lacrimans, Quis iam locus inquit Achate, <sup>460</sup>quae regio in terris nostri non plena laboris? <sup>461</sup>En Priamus! Sunt hic etiam sua praemia laudi; <sup>462</sup>sunt lacrimae rerum et mentem mortalia tangunt. <sup>463</sup>Solve metus; feret haec aliquam tibi fama salutem. <sup>464</sup>Sic ait, atque animum pictura pascit inani, <sup>465</sup>multa gemens, largoque umectat flumine voltum. <sup>466</sup>Namque videbat, uti bellantes Pergama circum <sup>467</sup>hac fugerent Graii, premeret Troiana iuventus, <sup>468</sup>hac Phryges, instaret curru cristatus Achilles. <sup>469</sup>Nec procul hinc Rhesi niveis tentoria velis <sup>470</sup>adgnoscit lacrimans, primo quae prodita somno <sup>471</sup>Tydides multa vastabat caede cruentus, <sup>472</sup>ardentisque avertit equos in castra, prius quam <sup>473</sup>pabula gustassent Troiae Xanthumque bibissent. <sup>474</sup>Parte alia fugiens amissis Troilus armis, <sup>475</sup>infelix puer atque impar congressus Achilli,
translation.text: His tender parent could no longer bear; But, interposing, sought to soothe his care. “Whoe’er you are, not unbelov’d by Heav’n, Since on our friendly shore your ships are driv’n: Have courage: to the gods permit the rest, And to the queen expose your just request. Now take this earnest of success, for more: Your scatter’d fleet is join’d upon the shore; The winds are chang’d, your friends from danger free; Or I renounce my skill in augury. Twelve swans behold in beauteous order move, And stoop with closing pinions from above; Whom late the bird of Jove had driv’n along, And thro’ the clouds pursued the scatt’ring throng: Now, all united in a goodly team, They skim the ground, and seek the quiet stream. As they, with joy returning, clap their wings, And ride the circuit of the skies in rings; Not otherwise your ships, and ev’ry friend, Already hold the port, or with swift sails descend. No more advice is needful; but pursue The path before you, and the town in view.”

translation.original_text: <sup>476</sup>fertur equis, curruque haeret resupinus inani, <sup>477</sup>lora tenens tamen; huic cervixque comaeque trahuntur <sup>478</sup>per terram, et versa pulvis inscribitur hasta. <sup>479</sup>Interea ad templum non aequae Palladis ibant <sup>480</sup>crinibus Iliades passis peplumque ferebant, <sup>481</sup>suppliciter tristes et tunsae pectora palmis; <sup>482</sup>diva solo fixos oculos aversa tenebat. <sup>483</sup>Ter circum Iliacos raptaverat Hectora muros, <sup>484</sup>exanimumque auro corpus vendebat Achilles. <sup>485</sup>Tum vero ingentem gemitum dat pectore ab imo, <sup>486</sup>ut spolia, ut currus, utque ipsum corpus amici, <sup>487</sup>tendentemque manus Priamum conspexit inermis. <sup>488</sup>Se quoque principibus permixtum adgnovit Achivis, <sup>489</sup>Eoasque acies et nigri Memnonis arma. <sup>490</sup>Ducit Amazonidum lunatis agmina peltis <sup>491</sup>Penthesilea furens, mediisque in milibus ardet, <sup>492</sup>aurea subnectens exsertae cingula mammae, <sup>493</sup>bellatrix, audetque viris concurrere virgo. <sup>494</sup>Haec dum Dardanio Aeneae miranda videntur, <sup>495</sup>dum stupet, obtutuque haeret defixus in uno, <sup>496</sup>regina ad templum, forma pulcherrima Dido, <sup>497</sup>incessit magna iuvenum stipante caterva. <sup>498</sup>Qualis in Eurotae ripis aut per iuga Cynthi <sup>499</sup>exercet Diana choros, quam mille secutae <sup>500</sup>hinc atque hinc glomerantur oreades; illa pharetram
translation.text:
Thus having said, she turn’d, and made appear Her neck refulgent, and dishevel’d hair, Which, flowing from her shoulders, reach’d the ground. And widely spread ambrosial scents around: In length of train descends her sweeping gown; And, by her graceful walk, the Queen of Love is known. The prince pursued the parting deity With words like these: “Ah! whither do you fly? Unkind and cruel! to deceive your son In borrow’d shapes, and his embrace to shun; Never to bless my sight, but thus unknown; And still to speak in accents not your own.” Against the goddess these complaints he made, But took the path, and her commands obey’d. They march, obscure; for Venus kindly shrouds With mists their persons, and involves in clouds, That, thus unseen, their passage none might stay, Or force to tell the causes of their way. This part perform’d, the goddess flies sublime To visit Paphos and her native clime; Where garlands, ever green and ever fair, With vows are offer’d, and with solemn pray’r: A hundred altars in her temple smoke; A thousand bleeding hearts her pow’r invoke.

They climb the next ascent, and, looking down, Now at a nearer distance view the town. The prince with wonder sees the stately tow’rs, Which late were huts and shepherds’ homely bow’rs, The gates and streets; and hears, from ev’ry part, The noise and busy concourse of the mart. The toiling Tyrians on each other call To ply their labour: some extend the wall; Some build the citadel; the brawny throng Or dig, or push unwieldly stones along. Some for their dwellings choose a spot of ground, Which, first design’d, with ditches they surround. Some laws ordain; and some attend the choice Of holy senates, and elect by voice. Here some design a mole, while others there Lay deep foundations for a theatre; From marble quarries mighty columns hew, For ornaments of scenes, and future view. Such is their toil, and such their busy pains, As exercise the bees in flow’ry plains, When winter past, and summer scarce begun, Invites them forth to labour in the sun; Some lead their youth abroad, while some condense Their liquid store, and some in cells dispense; Some at the gate stand ready to receive The golden burthen, and their friends relieve; All with united force, combine to drive The lazy drones from the laborious hive: With envy stung, they view each other’s deeds; The fragrant work with diligence proceeds. “Thrice happy you, whose walls already rise!” Aeneas said, and view’d, with lifted eyes, Their lofty tow’rs; then, ent’ring at the gate, Conceal’d in clouds (prodigious to relate) He mix’d, unmark’d, among the busy throng, Borne by the tide, and pass’d unseen along.

translation.original_text: <sup>501</sup>fert umero, gradiensque deas supereminet omnis: <sup>502</sup>Latonae tacitum pertemptant gaudia pectus: <sup>503</sup>talis erat Dido, talem se laeta ferebat <sup>504</sup>per medios, instans operi regnisque futuris. <sup>505</sup>Tum foribus divae, media testudine templi, <sup>506</sup>saepta armis, solioque alte subnixa resedit. <sup>507</sup>Iura dabat legesque viris, operumque laborem <sup>508</sup>partibus aequabat iustis, aut sorte trahebat: <sup>509</sup>cum subito Aeneas concursu accedere magno <sup>510</sup>Anthea Sergestumque videt fortemque Cloanthum, <sup>511</sup>Teucrorumque alios, ater quos aequore turbo <sup>512</sup>dispulerat penitusque alias avexerat oras. <sup>513</sup>Obstipuit simul ipse simul perculsus Achates <sup>514</sup>laetitiaque metuque; avidi coniungere dextras <sup>515</sup>ardebant; sed res animos incognita turbat. <sup>516</sup>Dissimulant, et nube cava speculantur amicti, <sup>517</sup>quae fortuna viris, classem quo litore linquant, <sup>518</sup>quid veniant; cunctis nam lecti navibus ibant, <sup>519</sup>orantes veniam, et templum clamore petebant. <sup>520</sup>Postquam introgressi et coram data copia fandi, <sup>521</sup>maxumus Ilioneus placido sic pectore coepit: <sup>522</sup>O Regina, novam cui condere Iuppiter urbem <sup>523</sup>iustitiaque dedit gentis frenare superbas, <sup>524</sup>Troes te miseri, ventis maria omnia vecti, <sup>525</sup>oramus, prohibe infandos a navibus ignis,
translation.text: Full in the centre of the town there stood, Thick set with trees, a venerable wood. The Tyrians, landing near this holy ground, And digging here, a prosp’rous omen found: From under earth a courser’s head they drew, Their growth and future fortune to foreshew. This fated sign their foundress Juno gave, Of a soil fruitful, and a people brave. Sidonian Dido here with solemn state Did Juno’s temple build, and consecrate, Enrich’d with gifts, and with a golden shrine; But more the goddess made the place divine. On brazen steps the marble threshold rose, And brazen plates the cedar beams inclose: The rafters are with brazen cov’rings crown’d; The lofty doors on brazen hinges sound. What first Aeneas in this place beheld, Reviv’d his courage, and his fear expell’d. For while, expecting there the queen, he rais’d His wond’ring eyes, and round the temple gaz’d, Admir’d the fortune of the rising town, The striving artists, and their arts’ renown; He saw, in order painted on the wall, Whatever did unhappy Troy befall: The wars that fame around the world had blown, All to the life, and ev’ry leader known. There Agamemnon, Priam here, he spies, And fierce Achilles, who both kings defies. He stopp’d, and weeping said: “O friend! ev’n here The monuments of Trojan woes appear! Our known disasters fill ev’n foreign lands: See there, where old unhappy Priam stands! Ev’n the mute walls relate the warrior’s fame, And Trojan griefs the Tyrians’ pity claim.” He said, his tears a ready passage find, Devouring what he saw so well design’d, And with an empty picture fed his mind: For there he saw the fainting Grecians yield, And here the trembling Trojans quit the field, Pursued by fierce Achilles thro’ the plain, On his high chariot driving o’er the slain. The tents of Rhesus next, his grief renew, By their white sails betray’d to nightly view; And wakeful Diomede, whose cruel sword The sentries slew, nor spar’d their slumb’ring lord, Then took the fiery steeds, ere yet the food Of Troy they taste, or drink the Xanthian flood. Elsewhere he saw where Troilus defied Achilles, and unequal combat tried; Then, where the boy disarm’d, with loosen’d reins, Was by his horses hurried o’er the plains, Hung by the neck and hair, and dragg’d around: The hostile spear, yet sticking in his wound, With tracks of blood inscrib’d the dusty ground. Meantime the Trojan dames, oppress’d with woe, To Pallas’ fane in long procession go, In hopes to reconcile their heav’nly foe. They weep, they beat their breasts, they rend their hair, And rich embroider’d vests for presents bear; But the stern goddess stands unmov’d with pray’r. Thrice round the Trojan walls Achilles drew The corpse of Hector, whom in fight he slew. Here Priam sues; and there, for sums of gold, The lifeless body of his son is sold. So sad an object, and so well express’d, Drew sighs and groans from the griev’d hero’s breast, To see the figure of his lifeless friend, And his old sire his helpless hand extend. Himself he saw amidst the Grecian train, Mix’d in the bloody battle on the plain; And swarthy Memnon in his arms he knew, His pompous ensigns, and his Indian crew. Penthisilea there, with haughty grace, Leads to the wars an Amazonian race: In their right hands a pointed dart they wield; The left, for ward, sustains the lunar shield. Athwart her breast a golden belt she throws, Amidst the press alone provokes a thousand foes, And dares her maiden arms to manly force oppose.

translation.original_text: <sup>526</sup>parce pio generi, et propius res aspice nostras. <sup>527</sup>Non nos aut ferro Libycos populare Penatis <sup>528</sup>venimus, aut raptas ad litora vertere praedas; <sup>529</sup>non ea vis animo, nec tanta superbia victis. <sup>530</sup>Est locus, Hesperiam Grai cognomine dicunt, <sup>531</sup>terra antiqua, potens armis atque ubere glaebae; <sup>532</sup>Oenotri coluere viri; nunc fama minores <sup>533</sup>Italiam dixisse ducis de nomine gentem. <sup>534</sup>Hic cursus fuit: <sup>535</sup>cum subito adsurgens fluctu nimbosus Orion <sup>536</sup>in vada caeca tulit, penitusque procacibus austris <sup>537</sup>perque undas, superante salo, perque invia saxa <sup>538</sup>dispulit; huc pauci vestris adnavimus oris. <sup>539</sup>Quod genus hoc hominum?  Quaeve hunc tam barbara morem <sup>540</sup>permittit patria? Hospitio prohibemur harenae; <sup>541</sup>bella cient, primaque vetant consistere terra. <sup>542</sup>Si genus humanum et mortalia temnitis arma <sup>543</sup>at sperate deos memores fandi atque nefandi. <sup>544</sup>Rex erat Aeneas nobis, quo iustior alter, <sup>545</sup>nec pietate fuit, nec bello maior et armis. <sup>546</sup>Quem si fata virum servant, si vescitur aura <sup>547</sup>aetheria, neque adhuc crudelibus occubat umbris, <sup>548</sup>non metus; officio nec te certasse priorem <sup>549</sup>paeniteat. Sunt et Siculis regionibus urbes <sup>550</sup>arvaque, Troianoque a sanguine clarus Acestes.
translation.text: Thus while the Trojan prince employs his eyes, Fix’d on the walls with wonder and surprise, The beauteous Dido, with a num’rous train And pomp of guards, ascends the sacred fane. Such on Eurotas’ banks, or Cynthus’ height, Diana seems; and so she charms the sight, When in the dance the graceful goddess leads The choir of nymphs, and overtops their heads: Known by her quiver, and her lofty mien, She walks majestic, and she looks their queen; Latona sees her shine above the rest, And feeds with secret joy her silent breast. Such Dido was; with such becoming state, Amidst the crowd, she walks serenely great. Their labour to her future sway she speeds, And passing with a gracious glance proceeds; Then mounts the throne, high plac’d before the shrine: In crowds around, the swarming people join. She takes petitions, and dispenses laws, Hears and determines ev’ry private cause; Their tasks in equal portions she divides, And, where unequal, there by lots decides. Another way by chance Aeneas bends His eyes, and unexpected sees his friends, Antheus, Sergestus grave, Cloanthus strong, And at their backs a mighty Trojan throng, Whom late the tempest on the billows toss’d, And widely scatter’d on another coast. The prince, unseen, surpris’d with wonder stands, And longs, with joyful haste, to join their hands; But, doubtful of the wish’d event, he stays, And from the hollow cloud his friends surveys, Impatient till they told their present state, And where they left their ships, and what their fate, And why they came, and what was their request; For these were sent, commission’d by the rest, To sue for leave to land their sickly men, And gain admission to the gracious queen. Ent’ring, with cries they fill’d the holy fane; Then thus, with lowly voice, Ilioneus began:

translation.original_text: <sup>551</sup>Quassatam ventis liceat subducere classem, <sup>552</sup>et silvis aptare trabes et stringere remos: <sup>553</sup>si datur Italiam, sociis et rege recepto, <sup>554</sup>tendere, ut Italiam laeti Latiumque petamus; <sup>555</sup>sin absumpta salus, et te, pater optume Teucrum, <sup>556</sup>pontus habet Lybiae, nec spes iam restat Iuli, <sup>557</sup>at freta Sicaniae saltem sedesque paratas, <sup>558</sup>unde huc advecti, regemque petamus Acesten. <sup>559</sup>Talibus Ilioneus; cuncti simul ore fremebant <sup>560</sup>Dardanidae. <sup>561</sup>Tum breviter Dido, voltum demissa, profatur: <sup>562</sup>Solvite corde metum, Teucri, secludite curas. <sup>563</sup>Res dura et regni novitas me talia cogunt <sup>564</sup>moliri, et late finis custode tueri. <sup>565</sup>Quis genus Aeneadum, quis Troiae nesciat urbem, <sup>566</sup>virtutesque virosque, aut tanti incendia belli? <sup>567</sup>Non obtusa adeo gestamus pectora Poeni, <sup>568</sup>nec tam aversus equos Tyria Sol iungit ab urbe. <sup>569</sup>Seu vos Hesperiam magnam Saturniaque arva, <sup>570</sup>sive Erycis finis regemque optatis Acesten, <sup>571</sup>auxilio tutos dimittam, opibusque iuvabo. <sup>572</sup>Voltis et his mecum pariter considere regnis; <sup>573</sup>urbem quam statuo vestra est, subducite navis; <sup>574</sup>Tros Tyriusque mihi nullo discrimine agetur. <sup>575</sup>Atque utinam rex ipse Noto compulsus eodem
translation.text: “O Queen! indulg’d by favour of the gods To found an empire in these new abodes, To build a town, with statutes to restrain The wild inhabitants beneath thy reign, We wretched Trojans, toss’d on ev’ry shore, From sea to sea, thy clemency implore. Forbid the fires our shipping to deface! Receive th’ unhappy fugitives to grace, And spare the remnant of a pious race! We come not with design of wasteful prey, To drive the country, force the swains away: Nor such our strength, nor such is our desire; The vanquish’d dare not to such thoughts aspire. A land there is, Hesperia nam’d of old; The soil is fruitful, and the men are bold Th’ Oenotrians held it once, by common fame Now call’d Italia, from the leader’s name. To that sweet region was our voyage bent, When winds and ev’ry warring element Disturb’d our course, and, far from sight of land, Cast our torn vessels on the moving sand: The sea came on; the South, with mighty roar, Dispers’d and dash’d the rest upon the rocky shore. Those few you see escap’d the storm, and fear, Unless you interpose, a shipwreck here. What men, what monsters, what inhuman race, What laws, what barb’rous customs of the place, Shut up a desert shore to drowning men, And drive us to the cruel seas again? If our hard fortune no compassion draws, Nor hospitable rights, nor human laws, The gods are just, and will revenge our cause. Aeneas was our prince: a juster lord, Or nobler warrior, never drew a sword; Observant of the right, religious of his word. If yet he lives, and draws this vital air, Nor we, his friends, of safety shall despair; Nor you, great queen, these offices repent, Which he will equal, and perhaps augment. We want not cities, nor Sicilian coasts, Where King Acestes Trojan lineage boasts. Permit our ships a shelter on your shores, Refitted from your woods with planks and oars, That, if our prince be safe, we may renew Our destin’d course, and Italy pursue. But if, O best of men, the Fates ordain That thou art swallow’d in the Libyan main, And if our young Iulus be no more, Dismiss our navy from your friendly shore, That we to good Acestes may return, And with our friends our common losses mourn.” Thus spoke Ilioneus: the Trojan crew With cries and clamours his request renew.

translation.original_text: <sup>576</sup>adforet Aeneas! Equidem per litora certos <sup>577</sup>dimittam et Libyae lustrare extrema iubebo, <sup>578</sup>si quibus eiectus silvis aut urbibus errat. <sup>579</sup>His animum arrecti dictis et fortis Achates <sup>580</sup>et pater Aeneas iamdudum erumpere nubem <sup>581</sup>ardebant. Prior Aenean compellat Achates: <sup>582</sup>Nate dea, quae nunc animo sententia surgit? <sup>583</sup>omnia tuta vides, classem sociosque receptos. <sup>584</sup>Unus abest, medio in fluctu quem vidimus ipsi <sup>585</sup>submersum; dictis respondent cetera matris. <sup>586</sup>Vix ea fatus erat, cum circumfusa repente <sup>587</sup>scindit se nubes et in aethera purgat apertum. <sup>588</sup>Restitit Aeneas claraque in luce refulsit, <sup>589</sup>os umerosque deo similis; namque ipsa decoram <sup>590</sup>caesariem nato genetrix lumenque iuventae <sup>591</sup>purpureum et laetos oculis adflarat honores: <sup>592</sup>quale manus addunt ebori decus, aut ubi flavo <sup>593</sup>argentum Pariusve lapis circumdatur auro. <sup>594</sup>Tum sic reginam adloquitur, cunctisque repente <sup>595</sup>improvisus ait: Coram, quem quaeritis, adsum, <sup>596</sup>Troius Aeneas, Lybicis ereptus ab undis. <sup>597</sup>O sola infandos Troiae miserata labores, <sup>598</sup>quae nos, reliquias Danaum, terraeque marisque <sup>599</sup>omnibus exhaustos iam casibus, omnium egenos, <sup>600</sup>urbe, domo, socias, grates persolvere dignas
translation.text:
The modest queen a while, with downcast eyes, Ponder’d the speech; then briefly thus replies: “Trojans, dismiss your fears; my cruel fate, And doubts attending an unsettled state, Force me to guard my coast from foreign foes. Who has not heard the story of your woes, The name and fortune of your native place, The fame and valour of the Phrygian race? We Tyrians are not so devoid of sense, Nor so remote from Phoebus’ influence. Whether to Latian shores your course is bent, Or, driv’n by tempests from your first intent, You seek the good Acestes’ government, Your men shall be receiv’d, your fleet repair’d, And sail, with ships of convoy for your guard: Or, would you stay, and join your friendly pow’rs To raise and to defend the Tyrian tow’rs, My wealth, my city, and myself are yours. And would to Heav’n, the Storm, you felt, would bring On Carthaginian coasts your wand’ring king. My people shall, by my command, explore The ports and creeks of ev’ry winding shore, And towns, and wilds, and shady woods, in quest Of so renown’d and so desir’d a guest.”

Rais’d in his mind the Trojan hero stood, And long’d to break from out his ambient cloud: Achates found it, and thus urg’d his way: “From whence, O goddess-born, this long delay? What more can you desire, your welcome sure, Your fleet in safety, and your friends secure? One only wants; and him we saw in vain Oppose the Storm, and swallow’d in the main. Orontes in his fate our forfeit paid; The rest agrees with what your mother said.” Scarce had he spoken, when the cloud gave way, The mists flew upward and dissolv’d in day.

translation.original_text: <sup>601</sup>non opis est nostrae, Dido, nec quicquid ubique est <sup>602</sup>gentis Dardaniae, magnum quae sparsa per orbem. <sup>603</sup>Di tibi, si qua pios respectant numina, si quid <sup>604</sup>usquam iustitia est et mens sibi conscia recti, <sup>605</sup>praemia digna ferant. Quae te tam laeta tulerunt <sup>606</sup>saecula? Qui tanti talem genuere parentes? <sup>607</sup>In freta dum fluvii current, dum montibus umbrae <sup>608</sup>lustrabunt convexa, polus dum sidera pascet, <sup>609</sup>semper honos nomenque tuum laudesque manebunt, <sup>610</sup>quae me cumque vocant terrae. Sic fatus, amicum <sup>611</sup>Ilionea petit dextra, laevaque Serestum, <sup>612</sup>post alios, fortemque Gyan fortemque Cloanthum. <sup>613</sup>Obstipuit primo aspectu Sidonia Dido, <sup>614</sup>casu deinde viri tanto, et sic ore locuta est: <sup>615</sup>Quis te, nate dea, per tanta pericula casus <sup>616</sup>insequitur? Quae vis immanibus applicat oris? <sup>617</sup>Tune ille Aeneas, quem Dardanio Anchisae <sup>618</sup>alma Venus Phrygii genuit Simoentis ad undam? <sup>619</sup>Atque equidem Teucrum memini Sidona venire <sup>620</sup>finibus expulsum patriis, nova regna petentem <sup>621</sup>auxilio Beli; genitor tum Belus opimam <sup>622</sup>vastabat Cyprum, et victor dicione tenebat. <sup>623</sup>Tempore iam ex illo casus mihi cognitus urbis <sup>624</sup>Troianae nomenque tuum regesque Pelasgi. <sup>625</sup>Ipse hostis Teucros insigni laude ferebat,
translation.text: The Trojan chief appear’d in open sight, August in visage, and serenely bright. His mother goddess, with her hands divine, Had form’d his curling locks, and made his temples shine, And giv’n his rolling eyes a sparkling grace, And breath’d a youthful vigour on his face; Like polish’d ivory, beauteous to behold, Or Parian marble, when enchas’d in gold: Thus radiant from the circling cloud he broke, And thus with manly modesty he spoke:

translation.original_text: <sup>626</sup>seque ortum antiqua Teucrorum ab stirpe volebat. <sup>627</sup>Quare agite, O tectis, iuvenes, succedite nostris. <sup>628</sup>Me quoque per multos similis fortuna labores <sup>629</sup>iactatam hac demum voluit consistere terra. <sup>630</sup>Non ignara mali, miseris succurrere disco. <sup>631</sup>Sic memorat; simul Aenean in regia ducit <sup>632</sup>tecta, simul divom templis indicit honorem. <sup>633</sup>Nec minus interea sociis ad litora mittit <sup>634</sup>viginti tauros, magnorum horrentia centum <sup>635</sup>terga suum, pinguis centum cum matribus agnos, <sup>636</sup>munera laetitiamque dii. <sup>637</sup>At domus interior regali splendida luxu <sup>638</sup>instruitur, mediisque parant convivia tectis: <sup>639</sup>arte laboratae vestes ostroque superbo, <sup>640</sup>ingens argentum mensis, caelataque in auro <sup>641</sup>fortia facta patrum, series longissima rerum <sup>642</sup>per tot ducta viros antiqua ab origine gentis. <sup>643</sup>Aeneas (neque enim patrius consistere mentem <sup>644</sup>passus amor) rapidum ad navis praemittit Achaten, <sup>645</sup>Ascanio ferat haec, ipsumque ad moenia ducat; <sup>646</sup>omnis in Ascanio cari stat cura parentis. <sup>647</sup>Munera praeterea, Iliacis erepta ruinis, <sup>648</sup>ferre iubet, pallam signis auroque rigentem, <sup>649</sup>et circumtextum croceo velamen acantho, <sup>650</sup>ornatus Argivae Helenae, quos illa Mycenis,
translation.text: “He whom you seek am I; by tempests toss’d, And sav’d from shipwreck on your Libyan coast; Presenting, gracious queen, before your throne, A prince that owes his life to you alone. Fair majesty, the refuge and redress Of those whom fate pursues, and wants oppress, You, who your pious offices employ To save the relics of abandon’d Troy; Receive the shipwreck’d on your friendly shore, With hospitable rites relieve the poor; Associate in your town a wand’ring train, And strangers in your palace entertain: What thanks can wretched fugitives return, Who, scatter’d thro’ the world, in exile mourn? The gods, if gods to goodness are inclin’d; If acts of mercy touch their heav’nly mind, And, more than all the gods, your gen’rous heart. Conscious of worth, requite its own desert! In you this age is happy, and this earth, And parents more than mortal gave you birth. While rolling rivers into seas shall run, And round the space of heav’n the radiant sun; While trees the mountain tops with shades supply, Your honour, name, and praise shall never die. Whate’er abode my fortune has assign’d, Your image shall be present in my mind.” Thus having said, he turn’d with pious haste, And joyful his expecting friends embrac’d: With his right hand Ilioneus was grac’d, Serestus with his left; then to his breast Cloanthus and the noble Gyas press’d; And so by turns descended to the rest.
